1: māśśāʾ Dammeśeq — the oracle against Damascus (the capital of Aram/Syria). mēsîr mēʿîr — "removed from being a city"; mĕʿî rûpâ — "a ruin heap/pile of ruins." ʿî and māpēlâ — these two terms for ruin are both used for urban devastation. Damascus fell to the Assyrians under Tiglath-pileser III in 732 BCE, which provides the historical context for this oracle.
2: The cities of Aroer (ʿārê ʿĔrôʿēr) — probably a region in Aram rather than the Aroer in Moab — are abandoned (ʿăzûbôt) to flocks. The imagery of wild animals taking over abandoned human habitations is a standard devastation-oracle feature (cf. Isa 13:20-22; 34:11-15). ʿēder — flock; the pastoral present replaces the urban past.
3: The fortified city (miḇṣār) ceases from Ephraim; the kingdom from Damascus. The oracle addresses the Syria-Ephraim coalition that threatened Ahaz in 734-735 BCE (cf. Isa 7:1-9). The remnant of Aram (šĕʾār ʾărām) will be like the glory of Israelites — both reduced to the same diminished state. The parallel fate of Israel and Syria is the theological point.
4: kĕbôd Yaʿăqōb yiddal — "the glory of Jacob will be diminished" — the contrast between Jacob's glory now and its diminished future state. mišman bĕśārô — "the fat of his flesh"; the emaciation metaphor (glory going from full to wasted) appears in famine and siege contexts. This is the judgment on the northern kingdom for its alliance with Syria against Judah.
5: kĕqôṣēr — "like a harvester" — the simile that controls vv.5-6. When someone gleans grain in the valley of Rephaim (ʿēmeq rĕpāʾîm, a fertile agricultural valley southwest of Jerusalem), they sweep the arm through the standing grain. The divine harvester sweeps through the population, leaving only a few.
6: bôṣēr (H1219) — gleaning/stripping; the remainder after the main harvest. The olive tree simile: two or three berries on the topmost bough (qōṣ-ʾōmer bērōʾš ʾāmîr) and four or five on the fruitful boughs after thorough beating. This is the "šĕʾār" — the remnant theology of Isaiah: judgment is total but not absolute; a remnant always survives. Cf. the detailed remnant theology of Isaiah 10:20-23.
7: bayyôm hahûʾ — "in that day" — the eschatological marker that introduces a reversal. yišʿeh haʾādām — "man will look to his maker" (ʿāśâhû, "the one who made him"). The judgment produces a return to proper orientation — the remnant will look to their creator rather than to their idols. This is the positive purpose of the divine pruning.
8: The things they will not look to: hammizbēḥôt (altars), maʿăśēh yādāyw (the work of their hands), and what their fingers have made (ʿāśû ʾeṣbĕʿōtāyw). The specific objects: hāʾăšērîm (Asherah poles — fertility goddess worship) and hĕḥammānîm (incense altars — associated with solar worship). These represent the full spectrum of Canaanite/syncretic religion that Isaiah consistently condemns.
9: The forsaken cities will be like ruins in the forests (kĕʿăzûbat hāḥōreš wĕhāʾāmîr) — like the abandoned places of the Hivites and Amorites who fled before Israel (though the text is uncertain). The point is reversal: just as Canaan was once emptied before Israel, now Israel's cities will be emptied before Assyria.
10: šākaḥt ʾĕlōhê yišʿēk — "you have forgotten the God of your salvation" — the core accusation. The people have planted naʿamānîm (pleasant/lovely plants, perhaps a term for sacred gardens for pagan deities) and set out zemôrat zār (slips of a foreign vine). The garden imagery may refer to ritual gardens dedicated to Adonis or another fertility deity. To plant for a foreign god is to forget one's own creator.
11: Though the plants are established (bĕyôm tiṭṭāʿennâ tĕśaggēj), the harvest will be gone on the day of disease and incurable pain. The sowing and harvest image is inverted: you planted but will not harvest — the deferred judgment that nullifies one's own effort. This reflects the covenant curse structure (Deut 28:30-31).
12: hôy hĕmôn — "woe to the tumult" — the escalating international noise. The simile: like the roar (šāʾôn) of many waters, like the rushing (šĕʾôn) of mighty nations. The hôy oracle is turned not against Israel now but against the nations massed against Zion — the subject shifts from Israel's sin to the nations' threat.
13: The nations rush forward like the rush of many waters — but YHWH rebukes (gāʿar) them and they flee far away (driven like chaff before the wind, like a rolling thing before a storm). kĕgalgal — a tumbleweed; lippnê-rûaḥ — before the wind. The contrast between the nations' threatening noise (v.12) and their actual fragility (v.13) is the rhetorical climax of the oracle.
14: lĕpĕnôt ʿereḇ — "at evening time" — the time of maximum threat. bĕṭerem bōqer ʾênennû — "before morning he is no more" — the sudden overnight reversal. This one-verse summary captures the entire dynamic of the chapter: threat (evening), divine intervention (overnight), extinction (morning). ḥēleq šōsēʾnû — "the portion of those who plunder us" — the theological conclusion that interprets the oracle as the fate of those who attack God's people.
